title: ukbflow
text: ukbflow 提供了一套完整的、RAP 原生的 UK Biobank 分析工作流 —— 从表型提取、疾病衍生，到关联分析和发表级图表，全程在 RAP 云端环境中运行。 UK Biobank 数据政策（2024+）：个体水平数据必须保留在 RAP 环境中，不得下载到本地。所有 ukbflow 函数均遵循此约束设计。
code: library(ukbflow)  # 本地生成合成 UKB 数据（在 RAP 上请替换为 extract_batch() + job_wait()） data <- ops_toy(n = 5000, seed = 2026) |>   derive_missing()  # 衍生肺癌结局（ICD-10 C34）及随访时间 data <- data |>   derive_icd10(name = "lung", icd10 = "C34",                source = c("cancer_registry", "hes")) |>   derive_followup(name         = "lung",                   event_col    = "lung_icd10_date",                   baseline_col = "p53_i0",                   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-10-31"),                   death_col    = "p40000_i0")  # 定义暴露变量：曾吸烟 vs. 从不吸烟 data[, smoking_ever := factor(   ifelse(p20116_i0 == "Never", "Never", "Ever"),   levels = c("Never", "Ever") )]  # Cox 回归：吸烟 → 肺癌（三模型校正框架） res <- assoc_coxph(data,   outcome_col  = "lung_icd10",   time_col     = "lung_followup_years",   exposure_col = "smoking_ever",   covariates   = c("p21022", "p31", "p22189"))  # 森林图 res_df <- as.data.frame(res) plot_forest(   data      = res_df,   est       = res_df$HR,   lower     = res_df$CI_lower,   upper     = res_df$CI_upper,   ci_column = 2L )

title: ukbflow
text: 环境要求： R ≥ 4.1 · dxpy（dx-toolkit，RAP 交互必需）
code: # 推荐 pak::pkg_install("evanbio/ukbflow")  # 或者 remotes::install_github("evanbio/ukbflow") pip install dxpy

title: ukbflow
text: auth_login()、auth_status()、auth_logout()、auth_list_projects()、auth_select_project() — RAP 认证 fetch_ls()、fetch_tree()、fetch_url()、fetch_file() — RAP 文件系统 fetch_metadata()、fetch_field() — UKB 元数据快捷下载 extract_ls()、extract_pheno()、extract_batch() — 表型提取 decode_values() — 整数编码 → 可读标签 decode_names() — 字段 ID → snake_case 列名 job_status() — 按 ID 查询任务状态 job_wait() — 阻塞等待任务完成（支持超时） job_path() — 获取已完成任务的输出路径 job_result() — 获取任务结果对象 job_ls() — 列出最近提交的任务 derive_missing() — 处理”不知道”/“不愿回答” derive_covariate() — 类型转换 + 分布汇总 derive_cut() — 连续变量分组 derive_selfreport() — 自报告疾病状态 + 日期 derive_hes() — HES 住院 ICD-10 derive_first_occurrence() — First Occurrence 字段 derive_cancer_registry() — 癌症注册 derive_death_registry() — 死亡注册 derive_icd10() — 多源合并（封装函数） derive_case() — 自报告 + ICD-10 最终合并 derive_timing() — 患病时间分类（现患 vs. 新发） derive_age() — 事件发生年龄 derive_followup() — 随访终止日期与随访年数 assoc_coxph() / assoc_cox() — Cox 比例风险模型（HR） assoc_logistic() / assoc_logit() — 逻辑回归（） assoc_linear() / assoc_lm() — 线性回归（β） assoc_coxph_zph() — 比例风险假设检验 assoc_subgroup() — 分层分析 + 交互项 LRT assoc_trend() — 剂量-反应趋势 + p_trend assoc_competing() — Fine-Gray 竞争风险（SHR） assoc_lag() — 滞后暴露敏感性分析 plot_forest() — 森林图（PNG / PDF / JPG / TIFF，300 dpi） plot_tableone() — 基线特征表（DOCX / HTML / PDF / PNG） ops_setup() — 环境健康检查（dx CLI、RAP 认证、R 包依赖） ops_toy() — 生成合成 UKB 风格数据，用于开发与测试 ops_na() — 逐列汇总缺失值（NA 与 ""）及缺失率 ops_snapshot() — 记录流程检查点，追踪数据集在各步骤的变化 ops_snapshot_cols() — 获取指定快照保存的列名列表 ops_snapshot_diff() — 比较两个快照之间的列差异 ops_snapshot_remove() — 删除某快照之后新增的列 ops_set_safe_cols() — 设置受保护列，ops_snapshot_remove 不会删除这些列 ops_withdraw() — 从队列中排除 UKB 撤回参与者 grs_check() — 验证 SNP 权重文件 grs_bgen2pgen() — 在 RAP 上将 BGEN 转换为 PGEN（提交云端任务） grs_score() — 使用 plink2 跨染色体计算 GRS grs_standardize() / grs_zscore() — Z 分标准化 grs_validate() — /HR per SD、高 vs. 低、趋势检验、AUC/C-index

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_* functions fit regression models exposure variable return tidy result tables suitable downstream forest plots publication tables. Prerequisite: analysis dataset already contain derived case status, follow-time, covariates produced derive_* functions. See vignette("derive") vignette("derive-survival").
code: library(ukbflow)  # ops_toy(scenario = "association") returns a pre-derived analysis-ready table: # covariates already as factors, bmi_cat / tdi_cat binned, and two outcomes # (dm_* and htn_*) with status / date / timing / followup columns in place. dt <- ops_toy(scenario = "association") dt <- dt[dm_timing != 1L]   # incident analysis: exclude prevalent DM cases

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: main functions automatically produce three adjustment levels without requiring manual formula construction: Age sex columns located automatically scanning dataset’s column names standard UKB naming patterns (p21022_* age recruitment, p31_* sex). fallback, column starting "age" named "sex" also recognised. prerequisite pipeline step required — detection works data.frame whose columns follow UKB ukbflow naming conventions. Set base = FALSE skip first two models run Fully adjusted model.

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_coxph() primary function time--event outcomes. accepts logical (TRUE/FALSE) integer (0/1) event indicators returns one row per exposure x term x model combination. Output columns: n, n_events, person_years reflect model-specific complete-case analysis set — participants missing value across outcome, time, exposure, covariates dropped fitting. counts therefore differ models (e.g. Fully adjusted typically fewer participants Unadjusted). | CI_lower, CI_upper | Confidence interval bounds | | p_value | Wald test p-value | | HR_label | Formatted string, e.g. "1.43 (1.28-1.60)" |
code: # Crude + age-sex adjusted (automatic); p21022 and p31 auto-detected res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = c("p20116_i0", "bmi_cat") ) # Add a Fully adjusted model res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   covariates   = c("bmi_cat", "tdi_cat", "p1558_i0",                    paste0("p22009_a", 1:10)) )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_logistic() binary outcomes without time dimension (e.g. case-control cross-sectional designs). sparse data small samples, use profile likelihood confidence intervals: Output identical structure assoc_coxph() OR_label place HR / HR_label, n_cases instead n_events / person_years.
code: res <- assoc_logistic(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   exposure_col = c("p20116_i0", "bmi_cat"),   covariates   = c("tdi_cat", "p1558_i0", paste0("p22009_a", 1:10)) ) res <- assoc_logistic(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   exposure_col = "grs_bmi",   ci_method    = "profile"   # slower but more accurate for sparse data )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_linear() continuous outcomes (e.g. biomarker levels, BMI). standard error beta included support downstream meta-analysis. Common mistake: passing binary (0/1) logical column outcome_col permitted (linear probability model) triggers warning. Use assoc_logistic() binary outcomes — linear regression 0/1 outcome produces unbounded predictions rarely appropriate epidemiological analyses.
code: # Continuous outcome (BMI); smoking and GRS as exposures res <- assoc_linear(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "p21001_i0",   exposure_col = c("p20116_i0", "grs_bmi"),   covariates   = c("tdi_cat", "p1558_i0", paste0("p22009_a", 1:10)) )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_coxph_zph() re-fits Cox models tests PH assumption via Schoenfeld residuals (cox.zph()). Use alongside assoc_coxph() validate model assumptions. Output columns include chisq, df, p_value, ph_satisfied (logical), global test statistics (global_chisq, global_df, global_p). PH assumption violated, consider adding strata() assoc_coxph() modelling time-varying effects.
code: zph <- assoc_coxph_zph(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = c("p20116_i0", "bmi_cat"),   covariates   = c("tdi_cat", "p1558_i0") )  # Identify any violations zph[ph_satisfied == FALSE]

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_subgroup() stratifies dataset grouping variable fits specified model within subgroup. likelihood ratio test (LRT) exposure x subgroup interaction computed full dataset appended p_interaction. Output columns include subgroup, subgroup_level, p_interaction addition standard effect estimate columns. Note: variable automatically excluded subgroup models. include covariates — collinearity warning issued . Subgroup analysis interpretable small number levels (e.g. sex, smoking status); continuous variables technically permitted per-unique-value subgrouping rarely meaningful practice — use pre-categorised version (e.g. via derive_cut()) instead.
code: # Subgroup by sex; p31 is automatically excluded from within-stratum models res <- assoc_subgroup(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   by           = "p31",   method       = "coxph",   covariates   = c("p21022", "bmi_cat", "tdi_cat") )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_trend() fits categorical trend models simultaneously ordered-factor exposure, returning per-category estimates alongside p-value linear trend. Supply custom scores reflecting approximate median BMI per category: output contains reference row (HR = 1.00 (ref)) followed non-reference rows, HR_per_score, HR_per_score_label, p_trend appended shared columns. trend columns carry value across every row within exposure × model combination — including reference row — full result table remains self-contained easy filter export.
code: # assoc_trend() requires an ordered factor; make bmi_cat ordered in-place dt[, bmi_cat := factor(bmi_cat, levels = levels(bmi_cat), ordered = TRUE)] res <- assoc_trend(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "bmi_cat",   method       = "coxph",   covariates   = c("p21022", "p31", "tdi_cat", "p20116_i0") ) res <- assoc_trend(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "bmi_cat",   method       = "coxph",   covariates   = c("p21022", "p31", "tdi_cat", "p20116_i0"),   scores       = c(17, 22, 27, 35)   # approximate median BMI per category )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_competing() fits Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard model via survival::finegray() + inverse-probability--censoring-weighted Cox regression. Use competing event (e.g. death) can preclude outcome interest. Two input modes supported: Mode — single column encodes event types: Mode B — separate 0/1 columns primary competing events: Output uses SHR (subdistribution hazard ratio) SHR_label place HR, adds n_compete (number competing events analysis set).
code: # Construct a combined event column: 0 = censored, 1 = DM, 2 = HTN (competing) dt_cr <- dt[htn_timing != 1L]   # also exclude prevalent HTN dt_cr[, event_type := data.table::fcase(   dm_timing  == 2L, 1L,   htn_timing == 2L, 2L,   default          = 0L )]  res <- assoc_competing(   data         = dt_cr,   outcome_col  = "event_type",       # 0 = censored, 1 = DM, 2 = HTN   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   event_val    = 1L,   compete_val  = 2L,   covariates   = c("bmi_cat", "tdi_cat") ) res <- assoc_competing(   data         = dt_cr,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",        # primary event   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = c("p20116_i0", "bmi_cat"),   compete_col  = "htn_status",       # competing event   covariates   = c("tdi_cat", "p1558_i0") )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: functions return data.table feeds directly plot_forest():
code: res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   covariates   = c("bmi_cat", "tdi_cat", "p1558_i0") )  # Pass directly to plot_forest() plot_forest(as.data.frame(res))  # Filter to a single model res[model == "Fully adjusted"]  # Export data.table::fwrite(res, "assoc_results.csv")

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: assoc_lag() re-runs Cox models one lag periods assess whether associations driven early events (reverse causation detection bias). lag, participants whose follow-time less lag_years excluded; follow-kept original scale. Setting lag_years = 0 (including 0 vector) runs model full unfiltered cohort, providing reference row lagged results can compared. Output adds two columns standard assoc_coxph() result:
code: res <- assoc_lag(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   lag_years    = c(0, 1, 2),   # 0 = full cohort reference   covariates   = c("bmi_cat", "tdi_cat", "p1558_i0") )

title: Association Analysis for UKB Outcomes
text: ?assoc_coxph, ?assoc_logistic, ?assoc_linear ?assoc_coxph_zph, ?assoc_subgroup, ?assoc_trend, ?assoc_competing, ?assoc_lag vignette("derive-survival") — follow-time event derivation vignette("derive") — disease phenotype derivation GitHub Issues

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: ukbflow interacts UK Biobank Research Analysis Platform (RAP) via DNAnexus dx toolkit. Authentication required access UKB project, submit jobs, retrieve results. Two working modes supported: Local → RAP: Run R locally, authenticate DNAnexus API token, interact RAP remotely. RAP → RAP: Run R inside RAP cloud environment (e.g., RStudio DNAnexus), authentication handled automatically platform.

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: Log DNAnexus platform Go Account Settings > API Tokens Click New Token, set appropriate expiry, copy token Keep token private. Treat like password — share commit version control.

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: recommended approach store token ~/.Renviron, local file never committed git. keeps token scripts entirely. restarting R, token available auth_login() automatically.
code: usethis::edit_r_environ() # Add the following line, then save and restart R: # DX_API_TOKEN=your_token_here

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: token cached locally dx toolkit persists across R sessions. local workflows, need log — subsequent R sessions usually require re-authentication unless token expires, local dx session cleared, explicitly log . prefer pass token directly (e.g., interactive session), can : Security note: Avoid saving auth_login("token") calls script files. Prefer .Renviron approach workflow intend save share.
code: library(ukbflow)  auth_login()  # reads DX_API_TOKEN from environment auth_login("your_token_here")

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: running inside RAP RStudio environment, authentication handled automatically platform. login step required:
code: library(ukbflow)  auth_status()  # verify the current session

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: auth_status() returns current user active project. Use confirm session running analysis.
code: auth_status() #> • User:    "user-XXXXXXXXXXXX" #> • Project: "project-XXXXXXXXXXXX"

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: returns RAP projects accessible account, along project IDs. Project IDs take form project-XXXXXXXXXXXX required auth_select_project().
code: auth_list_projects() #> project-XXXXXXXXXXXX : My UKB Project (CONTRIBUTOR) #> project-YYYYYYYYYYYY : Shared Analysis Project (VIEW)

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: ukbflow uses project IDs rather names avoid ambiguity. Use auth_list_projects() find correct ID first. selected, project context saved dx toolkit persists across sessions.
code: auth_select_project("project-XXXXXXXXXXXX") #> ✔ Project selected: "project-XXXXXXXXXXXX"

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: Logging invalidates current DNAnexus session remote platform. local token file removed becomes invalid. new token must generated DNAnexus platform logging .
code: auth_logout() #> ✔ Logged out from DNAnexus.

title: Authentication and Project Setup
text: DNAnexus API tokens limited validity period set creation time. token expires: auth_login() dx-dependent functions fail Generate new token DNAnexus platform log : update ~/.Renviron new token call auth_login() without arguments.
code: auth_login("your_new_token_here")

title: Authentication and Project Setup
code: library(ukbflow)  auth_login()                                 # authenticate auth_status()                                # verify session auth_list_projects()                         # find your project ID auth_select_project("project-XXXXXXXXXXXX")  # set active project  # ... run your analysis ...  auth_logout()                                # optional: clear session

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: Raw UKB phenotype data contains encoded column names values need converted analysis. outputs need decode_names() convert field ID column names human-readable snake_case. Call order matters: using extract_pheno() output, always run decode_values() decode_names(), value decoding relies numeric field ID still present column name.

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
code: library(ukbflow)  df <- extract_pheno(c(31, 54, 20116, 21022)) df <- decode_values(df)   # 0/1 → "Female"/"Male", etc. df <- decode_names(df)    # participant.p31 → sex

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: decode_values() converts raw integer codes human-readable labels categorical fields UKB encoding mappings. Continuous, date, text, already-decoded fields left unchanged. requires two metadata files UKB Showcase. Download : point decode_values() directory (default matches fetch_metadata()):
code: df <- decode_values(df) #> ✔ Decoded 3 categorical columns; 2 non-categorical columns unchanged. fetch_metadata(dest_dir = "data/metadata") df <- decode_values(df, metadata_dir = "data/metadata")

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: Codes absent encoding table (including UKB missing codes -1, -3, -7) returned NA.

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: decode_names() renames columns field ID format snake_case labels using approved UKB field dictionary available project.
code: df <- decode_names(df) #> ✔ Renamed 5 columns.

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: extract_pheno() format (participant.p31) extract_batch() format (p31) handled automatically.

title: Decoding UKB Column Names and Values
text: UKB field titles verbose. Names exceeding max_nchar characters flagged warning (default: 60). Lower threshold catch aggressively: Rename manually something concise:
code: df <- decode_names(df, max_nchar = 30) #> ! 1 column name longer than 30 characters - consider renaming manually: #> • date_of_attending_assessment_centre_i0 names(df)[names(df) == "date_of_attending_assessment_centre_i0"] <- "date_baseline"

title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: disease case definitions derived (see vignette("derive")), three additional functions prepare data time--event analysis: Prerequisite: {name}_status {name}_date must already present — produced vignette("derive"). examples assume full disease derivation pipeline run ops_toy() dataset, baseline date column p53_i0 age recruitment p21022.

what: Step 1: Classify Timing — Prevalent vs. Incident
title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: derive_timing() compares disease date UKB baseline assessment date assigns participant one four categories: Supply explicit column names defaults apply: Call per variable needed — example, combined case per individual source (HES, self-report, etc.).
code: library(ukbflow)  # Build on the derive pipeline from vignette("derive") df <- ops_toy(n = 500) df <- derive_missing(df) df <- derive_covariate(df, as_factor = c("p31", "p20116_i0")) df <- derive_selfreport(df, name = "dm", regex = "type 2 diabetes") df <- derive_icd10(df, name = "dm", icd10 = "E11", source = c("hes", "death")) df <- derive_case(df, name = "dm") # Uses {name}_status and {name}_date by default df <- derive_timing(df, name = "dm", baseline_col = "p53_i0") df <- derive_timing(df,   name         = "dm",   status_col   = "dm_status",   date_col     = "dm_date",   baseline_col = "p53_i0" )

title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: derive_age() computes age time event cases, returns NA non-cases cases without date. age_at_event=age_at_recruitment+event_date−baseline_date365.25\text{age\_at\_event} = \text{age\_at\_recruitment} + \frac{\text{event\_date} - \text{baseline\_date}}{365.25} divisor 365.25 accounts leap years, ensuring sub-monthly precision age calculation across full UKB follow-window. Supply explicit column mappings names follow default {name}_date / {name}_status pattern:
code: # Auto-detects {name}_date and {name}_status; produces age_at_{name} column. df <- derive_age(df,   name         = "dm",   baseline_col = "p53_i0",   age_col      = "p21022" ) df <- derive_age(df,   name         = "dm",   baseline_col = "p53_i0",   age_col      = "p21022",   date_cols    = c(dm = "dm_date"),   status_cols  = c(dm = "dm_status") )

title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: derive_followup() computes follow-end date earliest : outcome event date (participant case) Date death (field 40000; competing event) Date lost follow-(field 191) administrative censoring date Follow-time years derived baseline date. Output columns:
code: df <- derive_followup(df,   name         = "dm",   event_col    = "dm_date",   baseline_col = "p53_i0",   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-10-31"),   # set to your study's cut-off date   death_col    = "p40000_i0",   lost_col     = FALSE                    # not available in ops_toy )

what: Prevalent cases receive NA follow-up time
title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: Participants whose event date falls baseline date (prevalent cases, {name}_timing == 1) followup_years set NA rather zero negative value, meaning time--event analysis. Use derive_timing() identify exclude prevalent cases fitting Cox model (see full pipeline example ).

what: Auto-detection of death and lost-to-follow-up columns
title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: death_col lost_col NULL (default), derive_followup() looks automatically field cache (UKB fields 40000 191). Pass FALSE explicitly disable competing event:
code: df <- derive_followup(df,   name         = "dm",   event_col    = "dm_date",   baseline_col = "p53_i0",   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-10-31"),   death_col    = FALSE,   lost_col     = FALSE )

title: Survival Analysis Setup for UKB Outcomes
text: completing three steps, data contains everything needed fit Cox proportional hazards model: Column roles model:
code: library(survival)  # Incident analysis: exclude prevalent cases and those with undetermined timing df_incident <- df[dm_timing != 1L]  fit <- coxph(   Surv(dm_followup_years, dm_status) ~     p20116_i0 + p21022 + p31 + p1558_i0,   data = df_incident ) summary(fit)

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_* functions convert raw UKB columns analysis-ready variables. vignette covers disease phenotype derivation pipeline: functions accept data.frame data.table return data.table. data.table input, new columns added reference (copy); data.frame input converted data.table internally modification. production, replace ops_toy() extract_batch() followed decode_values() decode_names(). See vignette("decode"). Column names use RAP raw format (p{field}_{instance}_{array}) returned ops_toy() extract_batch() decoding.

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
code: library(ukbflow)  df <- ops_toy(n = 500)

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: UKB uses special labels "know" "Prefer answer" distinguish refusal true missing data. derive_missing() converts NA (default) retains "Unknown" modelling. Performance: derive_missing() uses data.table::set() -place replacement — column copies made regardless dataset size. keep non-response model category: add custom labels beyond built-list:
code: df <- derive_missing(df) df <- derive_missing(df, action = "unknown") df <- derive_missing(df, extra_labels = "Not applicable")

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_covariate() converts categorical columns factor prints distribution summary .
code: df <- derive_covariate(   df,   as_factor = c(     "p31",        # sex     "p20116_i0",  # smoking_status_i0     "p1558_i0"    # alcohol_intake_frequency_i0   ),   factor_levels = list(     p20116_i0 = c("Never", "Previous", "Current")   ) )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_cut() creates new factor column binning continuous variable quantile-based custom groups.
code: df <- derive_cut(   df,   col    = "p21001_i0",                              # body_mass_index_bmi_i0   n      = 4,   breaks = c(18.5, 25, 30),   labels = c("Underweight", "Normal", "Overweight", "Obese"),   name   = "bmi_cat" )  df <- derive_cut(   df,   col    = "p22189",                                 # townsend_deprivation_index_at_recruitment   n      = 4,   labels = c("Q1 (least deprived)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (most deprived)"),   name   = "tdi_cat" )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_selfreport() searches UKB self-reported non-cancer illness (field 20002) cancer (field 20001) columns disease label matching regex, returns binary status earliest report date. Column detection automatic field IDs. adds two columns per call:
code: # Non-cancer: type 2 diabetes (field 20002) df <- derive_selfreport(df,   name  = "dm",   regex = "type 2 diabetes" ) # Cancer: lung cancer (field 20001) df <- derive_selfreport(df,   name  = "lung_cancer",   regex = "lung cancer",   field = "cancer" )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_hes() scans UKB Hospital Episode Statistics ICD-10 codes (field 41270, stored JSON array per participant) matches earliest corresponding date field 41280. match argument controls codes compared:
code: # Prefix match: codes starting with "I10" (hypertension) df <- derive_hes(df, name = "htn", icd10 = "I10")  # Exact match df <- derive_hes(df, name = "dm_hes", icd10 = "E11", match = "exact")  # Regex: E10 and E11 simultaneously df <- derive_hes(df, name = "dm_broad", icd10 = "^E1[01]", match = "regex")

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: UKB First Occurrence fields (p131xxx) record earliest date condition observed across linked sources — self-report, HES inpatient, GP records, death registry — pre-integrated UKB. Look disease UKB Field Finder.
code: # ops_toy includes p131742 as a representative First Occurrence column df <- derive_first_occurrence(df, name = "htn", field = 131742L, col = "p131742")

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_cancer_registry() searches cancer registry ICD-10 field (40006) optionally filters histology (field 40011) behaviour (field 40012).
code: # ICD-10 only df <- derive_cancer_registry(df,   name  = "skin_cancer",   icd10 = "^C44" )  # With histology and behaviour filters df <- derive_cancer_registry(df,   name      = "scc",   icd10     = "^C44",   histology = c(8070L, 8071L, 8072L),   behaviour = 3L                        # 3 = malignant )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_death_registry() searches primary (field 40001) secondary (field 40002) causes death ICD-10 codes.
code: df <- derive_death_registry(df, name = "mi",   icd10 = "I21") df <- derive_death_registry(df, name = "dm",   icd10 = "E11") df <- derive_death_registry(df, name = "lung", icd10 = "C34")

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_icd10() high-level wrapper calls combination source-specific functions merges outputs single status column earliest date. recommended approach multi-source ascertainment. Intermediate source columns retained alongside combined result:
code: # Non-cancer disease: HES + death + First Occurrence df <- derive_icd10(df,   name   = "dm",   icd10  = "E11",   source = c("hes", "death", "first_occurrence"),   fo_col = "p131742" )  # Cancer outcome: cancer registry df <- derive_icd10(df,   name      = "lung",   icd10     = "^C3[34]",   match     = "regex",   source    = "cancer_registry",   behaviour = 3L )

title: Deriving Disease Phenotypes from UKB Data
text: derive_case() merges self-report ICD-10 flags unified case status, earliest date across sources taken via pmin(). Output columns: earliest date matters: dm_date direct input derive_timing(), derive_age(), derive_followup() — chronological anchor every downstream survival analysis. See vignette("derive-survival").
code: df <- derive_case(df, name = "dm")

title: Extracting Phenotype Data
text: UKB phenotype data stored proprietary .dataset format RAP read directly. extract_* functions provide R interfaces discovering approved fields extracting phenotype data via DNAnexus dx extract_dataset table-exporter tools. Two workflows available: extract_batch() recommended approach serious analysis. extract_pheno() provided quick interactive inspection inside RAP environment .

title: Extracting Phenotype Data
text: Ensure authenticated selected project:
code: library(ukbflow)  auth_login() auth_select_project("project-XXXXXXXXXXXX")

title: Extracting Phenotype Data
text: extracting, use extract_ls() explore fields approved project: result data.frame two columns: Fields reflect project’s approved data — UKB fields present.
code: # List all approved fields (cached after first call) extract_ls()  # Search by keyword extract_ls(pattern = "cancer") extract_ls(pattern = "p31|p53|p21022")  # Force refresh after switching projects or datasets extract_ls(refresh = TRUE)

title: Extracting Phenotype Data
text: large-scale production extractions, submit asynchronous table-exporter job RAP cloud: job runs asynchronously RAP cloud. output CSV saved RAP project can monitored job_ series:
code: # Submit extraction job job_id <- extract_batch(c(31, 53, 21022, 22189))  # Custom output name job_id <- extract_batch(   field_id = c(31, 53, 21022, 22189),   file     = "ukb_demographics" )  # High priority (faster queue, higher cost) job_id <- extract_batch(   field_id = c(31, 53, 21022, 22189),   priority = "high" ) job_status(job_id)        # check progress job_path(job_id)          # get cloud file path once complete job_result(job_id)        # read result as data.table (inside RAP only)

title: Extracting Phenotype Data
text: extract_batch() automatically selects appropriate instance based number columns: can override instance_type argument needed.

title: Extracting Phenotype Data
text: small-scale interactive checks inside RAP RStudio environment: extract_pheno() restricted RAP environment returns data memory . analysis intended saved reproduced, use extract_batch(). Note: extract_pheno() returns raw coded values (e.g. 1/0 Sex, numeric codes diseases). Use decode_* series convert codes human-readable labels.
code: df <- extract_pheno(c(31, 53, 21022))

title: Exploring and Fetching RAP Files
text: fetch_* functions provide convenient R interface exploring downloading files UK Biobank RAP project. Rather switching terminal using dx commands directly, can browse remote project structure retrieve files entirely within R session. UK Biobank Data Policy (2024+): summary-level outputs metadata files may downloaded locally. Individual-level phenotype genotype data must remain within RAP environment.

title: Exploring and Fetching RAP Files
text: Ensure authenticated using fetch_* functions: See vignette("auth") details.
code: library(ukbflow)  auth_login() auth_select_project("project-XXXXXXXXXXXX")

title: Exploring and Fetching RAP Files
text: fetch_ls() lists contents remote RAP directory, returning structured data frame: returned data frame four columns:
code: # List project root fetch_ls() #>                  name   type    size            modified #> 1  Showcase metadata folder    <NA>                <NA> #> 2             results folder    <NA>                <NA> #> 3        analysis.log   file  4.2 KB 2024-11-01 10:22:03  # List a specific folder fetch_ls("Showcase metadata/") #>          name  type     size            modified #> 1   field.tsv  file  12.3 MB 2024-10-15 08:01:44 #> 2 esimpint.tsv  file   3.1 MB 2024-10-15 08:01:50  # Filter by type fetch_ls("results/", type = "file")  # Filter by name pattern fetch_ls("results/", pattern = "\\.csv$")

title: Exploring and Fetching RAP Files
text: fetch_tree() prints tree-like view remote project structure: Note: level recursion triggers one API call per folder. Keep max_depth 2–3 interactive use avoid long waits large projects.
code: # Top-level overview fetch_tree()  # Drill into a subfolder fetch_tree("results/", max_depth = 2)

title: Exploring and Fetching RAP Files
text: fetch_url() generates temporary pre-authenticated HTTPS URLs remote files. Useful passing downstream tools scripting metadata results workflows without triggering full download. URLs valid specified duration (default: "1d").
code: # Single file fetch_url("Showcase metadata/field.tsv")  # Entire folder (returns a named character vector) fetch_url("Showcase metadata/", duration = "7d")

title: Exploring and Fetching RAP Files
text: fetch_file() downloads file entire folder current specified directory within RAP environment. Note: fetch_file(), fetch_metadata(), fetch_field() can called within RAP environment. Calling locally produce error, individual-level UKB data must remain platform. Folders downloaded parallel using curl::multi_download() efficiency.
code: # Download a single file fetch_file("Showcase metadata/field.tsv", dest_dir = "data/")  # Download an entire folder fetch_file("Showcase metadata/", dest_dir = "data/metadata/")  # Resume an interrupted download fetch_file("results/summary_stats.csv", dest_dir = "data/", resume = TRUE)

title: Exploring and Fetching RAP Files
text: Two convenience wrappers provided commonly used UKB files:
code: # Download all Showcase metadata files (field.tsv, encoding.tsv, etc.) fetch_metadata(dest_dir = "data/metadata")  # Download the field dictionary only fetch_field(dest_dir = "data/metadata")

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: grs_* functions provide end--end pipeline computing validating polygenic / genetic risk scores within UK Biobank Research Analysis Platform (RAP). individual-level genotype data downloaded locally, computationally intensive steps executed cloud jobs via DNAnexus Swiss Army Knife app. Typical pipeline: Prerequisite: must authenticated UKB RAP project selected. See vignette("auth").
code: grs_check()  ->  grs_bgen2pgen()  ->  grs_score()  ->  grs_standardize()  ->  grs_validate()

what: Step 1: Validate the Weights File – grs_check()
title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: uploading RAP, validate SNP weights file. function reads file, checks required columns, flags problems, writes plink2-compatible space-delimited output. Required columns: validated weights also returned invisibly inspection.
code: library(ukbflow)  # Local: weights file on your machine w <- grs_check("weights.csv", dest = "weights_clean.txt") #> Read 'weights.csv': 312 rows, 5 columns. #> ✔ No NA values. #> ✔ No duplicate SNPs. #> ✔ All SNP IDs match rs[0-9]+ format. #> ✔ All effect alleles are A/T/C/G. #> Beta summary: #>   Range     : -0.412 to 0.589 #>   Mean |beta|: 0.183 #>   Positive  : 187 (59.9%) #>   Negative  : 125 (40.1%) #>   Zero      : 0 #> ✔ Weights file passed checks: 312 SNPs ready for UKB RAP. #> ✔ Saved: 'weights_clean.txt' # On RAP (RStudio) -- use /mnt/project/ paths directly w <- grs_check(   file = "/mnt/project/weights/weights.csv",   dest = "/mnt/project/weights/weights_clean.txt" )

what: Step 2: Convert BGEN to PGEN – grs_bgen2pgen()
title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: UKB imputed genotype data stored BGEN format RAP. grs_bgen2pgen() submits one Swiss Army Knife job per chromosome convert BGEN files faster PGEN format MAF filter applied via plink2. function called local machine RAP RStudio – heavy lifting runs entirely cloud. Instance types: Key arguments: Storage warning: chromosomes 1–16 may exceed 200 GB SSD "standard" instances. Use instance = "large" .
code: # Test on the smallest chromosome first ids <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 22, dest = "/pgen/", priority = "high") #> Uploading 'grs_bgen2pgen_std.R' to RAP ... #> ✔ Uploaded: '/grs_bgen2pgen_std.R' #> Submitting 1 job(s) -- mem2_ssd1_v2_x4 / priority: high #> ✔ chr22 -> 'job-Gxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx' #> ✔ 1/1 job(s) submitted. Monitor with job_ls(). # Full run: small chromosomes on standard, large on upgraded instance ids_small <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 17:22, dest = "/pgen/") ids_large <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 1:16,  dest = "/pgen/", instance = "large")  # Monitor progress (job_wait() takes one job ID at a time) job_ls() for (id in c(ids_small, ids_large)) job_wait(id)

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: PGEN files ready, grs_score() uploads weights file(s) submits one plink2 scoring job per GRS. job scores 22 chromosomes saves single CSV RAP. running RAP RStudio weights already project root, upload step skipped automatically: Important: maf argument must match value used grs_bgen2pgen() correct PGEN files located. Output per job: /grs/<GRS_name>_scores.csv columns IID GRS score named GRS_<name>.
code: ids <- grs_score(   file     = c(     grs_a = "weights/grs_a_weights.txt",     grs_b = "weights/grs_b_weights.txt"   ),   pgen_dir = "/mnt/project/pgen",   dest     = "/grs/",   priority = "high" ) #> ── Uploading 2 weight file(s) to RAP ──────────────────────────────────────── #> Uploading 'grs_a_weights.txt' ... #> ✔ Uploaded: '/grs_a_weights.txt' #> Uploading 'grs_b_weights.txt' ... #> ✔ Uploaded: '/grs_b_weights.txt' #> Submitting 2 job(s) -- mem2_ssd1_v2_x4 / priority: high #> ✔ grs_a -> 'job-Gxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx' #> ✔ grs_b -> 'job-Gyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyy' #> ✔ 2/2 job(s) submitted. Monitor with job_ls().  job_wait(ids) # On RAP: weights already at /mnt/project/grs_a_weights.txt ids <- grs_score(   file     = c(grs_a = "/mnt/project/grs_a_weights.txt"),   pgen_dir = "/mnt/project/pgen",   dest     = "/grs/" ) #> ℹ grs_a_weights.txt already at RAP root, skipping upload.

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: downloading score CSVs RAP (via fetch_file()) merging analysis dataset, Z-score standardise GRS column effect estimates interpretable per-SD associations. grs_zscore() alias produces identical result. original columns preserved; _z columns inserted immediately source column.
code: # Auto-detect all columns containing "grs" (case-insensitive) cohort <- grs_standardize(cohort) #> Auto-detected 2 GRS column(s): 'GRS_a', 'GRS_b' #> ✔ GRS_a -> GRS_a_z  [mean=0.0000, sd=1.0000] #> ✔ GRS_b -> GRS_b_z  [mean=0.0000, sd=1.0000] # Or specify columns explicitly cohort <- grs_standardize(cohort, grs_cols = c("GRS_a", "GRS_b"))

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: grs_validate() runs four complementary validation analyses GRS:

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
text: Return value: named list four data.table elements. AUC calculation requires pROC package. Install install.packages("pROC") needed.
code: res <- grs_validate(   data        = cohort,   grs_cols    = c("GRS_a_z", "GRS_b_z"),   outcome_col = "outcome",   time_col    = "followup_years",   covariates  = c("age", "sex", paste0("pc", 1:10)) )  res$per_sd          # HR per SD x model res$high_vs_low     # HR: top 20% vs bottom 20% res$trend           # p-trend across Q1–Q4 res$discrimination  # C-index with 95% CI

title: Genetic Risk Score (GRS) Analysis on UKB RAP
code: library(ukbflow)  # 1. Validate weights (local) grs_check("weights.csv", dest = "weights_clean.txt")  # 2. Convert BGEN -> PGEN on RAP (submit jobs) ids_std  <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 17:22, dest = "/pgen/", maf = 0.01) ids_lrg  <- grs_bgen2pgen(chr = 1:16,  dest = "/pgen/", maf = 0.01, instance = "large") for (id in c(ids_std, ids_lrg)) job_wait(id)  # 3. Score GRS on RAP (submit jobs) score_ids <- grs_score(   file     = c(grs_a = "weights_clean.txt"),   pgen_dir = "/mnt/project/pgen",   maf      = 0.01,   # must match grs_bgen2pgen()   dest     = "/grs/" ) job_wait(score_ids)  # 4. Download score CSV from RAP fetch_file("/grs/GRS_a_scores.csv", dest = "GRS_a_scores.csv")  # 5. Merge into analysis dataset and standardise # cohort: your analysis data.table with IID and phenotype columns scores <- data.table::fread("GRS_a_scores.csv")   # columns: IID, GRS_a cohort <- scores[cohort, on = "IID"]               # right-join: keep all cohort rows cohort <- grs_standardize(cohort, grs_cols = "GRS_a")  # 6. Validate res <- grs_validate(   data        = cohort,   grs_cols    = "GRS_a_z",   outcome_col = "outcome",   time_col    = "followup_years",   covariates  = c("age", "sex", paste0("pc", 1:10)) )  res$per_sd res$discrimination

title: Installation Guide
text: ukbflow R package UK Biobank data analysis visualization, designed work natively UK Biobank Research Analysis Platform (RAP). supports two working modes: Local → RAP: Run R locally, authenticate DNAnexus, submit jobs, download summary results. RAP → RAP: Run R directly inside RAP cloud environment, authentication handled automatically. UK Biobank Data Policy (2024+): Individual-level phenotype genotype data must remain within RAP environment downloaded locally. summary-level outputs (aggregated statistics, plots, reports) may exported.

title: Monitoring and Retrieving Extraction Jobs
text: extract_batch() submits table-exporter job, runs asynchronously RAP cloud. job_* functions let monitor progress, inspect job history, load results job completes.

title: Monitoring and Retrieving Extraction Jobs
code: library(ukbflow)  # 1. Submit extraction job job_id <- extract_batch(c(31, 53, 21022, 22189), file = "ukb_demographics")  # 2. Wait for completion job_wait(job_id)  # 3. Load result (RAP only) df <- job_result(job_id)

title: Monitoring and Retrieving Extraction Jobs
text: job_status() returns current state job: Possible states: failed jobs, error message accessible via:
code: job_status(job_id) #> job-XXXXXXXXXXXX #>            done s <- job_status(job_id) if (s == "failed") cli::cli_inform(attr(s, "failure_message"))

title: Monitoring and Retrieving Extraction Jobs
text: job_wait() polls regular intervals job reaches terminal state: job_wait() stops error job fails terminated, can safely chain job_result():
code: job_wait(job_id)                    # wait indefinitely (default) job_wait(job_id, interval = 60)     # poll every 60 seconds job_wait(job_id, timeout = 7200)    # give up after 2 hours job_wait(job_id) df <- job_result(job_id)

title: Monitoring and Retrieving Extraction Jobs
text: job_path() returns /mnt/project/ path output CSV RAP: Use read file directly pass tools:
code: path <- job_path(job_id) #> "/mnt/project/results/ukb_demographics.csv" df <- data.table::fread(job_path(job_id))

title: Monitoring and Retrieving Extraction Jobs
text: job_result() combines job_path() fread() one step. Must run inside RAP environment:
code: df <- job_result(job_id) # returns a data.table, e.g. 502353 rows x 5 cols (incl. eid)

title: Monitoring and Retrieving Extraction Jobs

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: ops_* functions set lightweight utilities sit outside main analysis pipeline. help verify environment starting, explore data quality, track cohort changes processing step. ops_setup() may query dx CLI RAP authentication status part health check. functions operate entirely locally: ops_toy() ops_na() read-; ops_snapshot() companions track optionally clean columns; ops_withdraw() removes withdrawn participants -place. None read write RAP storage.

RAP authentication ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ✔ user: evan.zhou #> ✔ project: project-GXk9... #> ── 3. R packages ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ✔ cli  3.6.3  [core] #> ✔ data.table  1.15.4  [core] #> ✔ survival  3.7.0  [assoc_coxph] #> ✔ forestploter  1.1.1  [plot_forest] #> ... #> ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ✔ 15 passed #> ! 2 optional / warning result <- ops_setup(verbose = FALSE) result$summary #> $pass #> [1] 15 #> $warn #> [1] 2 #> $fail #> [1] 0  # Gate the rest of your script on a clean environment stopifnot(result$summary$fail == 0) # Check R package dependencies only (skip dx and RAP auth) ops_setup(check_dx = FALSE, check_auth = FALSE)

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: ops_toy() generates realistic entirely synthetic dataset mimics structure UKB phenotype data RAP. Use develop test derive_*, assoc_*, plot_* functions without needing real UKB data access.

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: default "cohort" scenario produces wide participant-level table covers major UKB data domains: Column groups included: messy columns deliberately stress-test derive_missing() ops_na() common data quality issues (-NA columns, empty strings, non-standard missing labels). Feed output directly derive pipeline:
code: dt <- ops_toy() #> ✔ ops_toy: 1000 participants | 75 columns | scenario = "cohort" | seed = 42  dim(dt) #> [1] 1000   75  names(dt) #>  [1] "eid"          "p31"          "p34"          "p53_i0" #>  [5] "p21022"       "p21001_i0"    "p20116_i0"    "p1558_i0" #>  ... dt <- ops_toy() dt <- derive_missing(dt) dt <- derive_covariate(dt,   as_numeric = "p21001_i0",   as_factor  = c("p31", "p20116_i0") )

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: "forest" scenario returns results table matching output assoc_coxph(), useful developing testing plot_forest() without running real Cox model:
code: dt_forest <- ops_toy(scenario = "forest") #> ✔ ops_toy: 24 rows | 11 columns | scenario = "forest" | seed = 42  plot_forest(   data  = dt_forest[model == "Fully adjusted"],   est   = dt_forest[model == "Fully adjusted", HR],   lower = dt_forest[model == "Fully adjusted", CI_lower],   upper = dt_forest[model == "Fully adjusted", CI_upper] )

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: Results reproducible default (seed = 42). Pass seed = NULL different dataset every call:
code: dt1 <- ops_toy(seed = 1) dt2 <- ops_toy(seed = 1) identical(dt1, dt2)   # TRUE  dt_random <- ops_toy(seed = NULL)   # different every call

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: ops_na() scans every column NA empty strings (""), returning counts percentages sorted missingness. Counting "" missing intentional — UKB exports frequently use empty strings placeholders absent text values, ops_na() reports effective missingness rather plain .na() count. designed called derive_missing() understand data quality profile freshly extracted UKB dataset. Columns ≥ 10% missing flagged red (✖); 0% 10% yellow (!). summary block (totals) always printed regardless threshold setting.

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: Use threshold silence low-missingness columns per-column listing dataset many columns. summary block returned data.table always complete.
code: # Only list columns with > 50% missing in the console output ops_na(dt, threshold = 50)  # Suppress all per-column lines — summary only ops_na(dt, threshold = 99)

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: ops_na() returns data.table invisibly, regardless threshold:
code: result <- ops_na(dt, verbose = FALSE) result #>           column  n_na pct_na #>           <char> <int>  <num> #>  1:  messy_allna  1000  100.0 #>  2:    p41280_a4  1000  100.0 #>  ...  # Identify columns to drop before modelling cols_to_drop <- result[pct_na > 90, column] dt[, (cols_to_drop) := NULL]

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: label omitted, snapshots named snapshot_1, snapshot_2, etc. automatically. Labels unique within session: label used twice, history row appended stored column list overwritten — can cause ops_snapshot_cols() ops_snapshot_diff() behave unexpectedly.

what: ops_snapshot_cols() — column names at a checkpoint
title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: Returns column names recorded given snapshot label, minus protected columns (eid, sex, age, age_at_recruitment, registered via ops_set_safe_cols()). primary use building drop vector raw columns longer needed. Pass keep protect additional columns beyond defaults:
code: raw_cols <- ops_snapshot_cols("raw") # raw_cols is a character vector of droppable column names raw_cols <- ops_snapshot_cols("raw", keep = "p53_i0")

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: Returns lists columns added removed two snapshots — useful auditing derive_* functions produced.
code: result <- ops_snapshot_diff("raw", "after_derive_missing") result$added    # columns added in this step result$removed  # columns dropped in this step

what: ops_snapshot_remove() — drop raw columns after deriving
title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: Removes raw columns captured snapshot data, keeping derived columns added since. Built-safe columns (eid, etc.) columns supplied keep always retained. data.table input operation reference (-place); data.frame input new data.table returned original modified.
code: # After deriving, drop the original raw columns dt <- ops_snapshot_remove(dt, from = "raw") #> ✔ ops_snapshot_remove: dropped 60 raw columns, 15 remaining.

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: Adds column names session safe list never dropped ops_snapshot_cols() ops_snapshot_remove().
code: ops_set_safe_cols(c("date_baseline", "age_at_recruitment"))  # Clear registered safe cols ops_set_safe_cols(reset = TRUE)

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: UK Biobank periodically issues withdrawal files listing participants revoked consent. ops_withdraw() reads headerless single-column CSV supplied UKB removes matching rows dataset. Two snapshots (before_withdraw / after_withdraw) recorded automatically. Run immediately loading extracted dataset, derive_* steps, withdrawn participants never enter analysis.
code: dt <- ops_withdraw(dt, file = "withdraw.csv") #> ── snapshot: before_withdraw ─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   rows      502,492 #>   ... #> ── snapshot: after_withdraw ──────────────────────────────────────────────── #>   rows      502,489  (-3) #>   ... #> ℹ Withdrawal file: w854944_20260310.csv (312 IDs) #> ✖ Excluded: 3 participants found in data #> ✔ Remaining: 502,489 participants

title: Operational Utilities: Setup, Diagnostics, and Pipeline Tracking
text: four ops_* functions form natural bookend around core pipeline:
code: library(ukbflow)  # 1. Verify environment before starting ops_setup()  # 2. Generate test data (or extract real data from RAP) dt <- ops_toy()  # 3. Inspect data quality before processing ops_na(dt)  # 4. Run pipeline with checkpoints ops_snapshot(dt, label = "raw")  dt <- derive_missing(dt) ops_snapshot(dt, label = "after_derive_missing")  dt <- derive_covariate(dt,   as_numeric = "p21001_i0",   as_factor  = c("p31", "p20116_i0") ) ops_snapshot(dt, label = "after_derive_covariate")  # 5. Review full pipeline history ops_snapshot()

title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: Two functions produce publication-ready figures tables minimal post-processing: save = TRUE, functions write supported formats single call return plot/table object invisibly customisation.

title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: plot_forest() takes data frame whose first column row label, plus additional display columns. CI graphic formatted (95% CI) text column inserted automatically.
code: library(ukbflow)  df <- data.frame(   item      = c("Exposure vs. control", "Unadjusted", "Fully adjusted"),   `Cases/N` = c("", "89 / 4 521", "89 / 4 521"),   p_value   = c(NA_real_, 0.001, 0.006),   check.names = FALSE )  p <- plot_forest(   data      = df,   est       = c(NA,   1.52, 1.43),   lower     = c(NA,   1.18, 1.11),   upper     = c(NA,   1.96, 1.85),   ci_column = 3L,   indent    = c(0L,   1L,   1L),   p_cols    = "p_value",   xlim      = c(0.5,  3.0) ) plot(p)

what: Building the input data frame from assoc_*() results
title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: output assoc_coxph() (siblings) can reshaped directly format expected plot_forest():
code: dt  <- ops_toy(scenario = "association") dt  <- dt[dm_timing != 1L]  res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   covariates   = c("bmi_cat", "tdi_cat", "p1558_i0") ) res <- as.data.frame(res)  # Reshape: one row per model, label column first df2 <- data.frame(   item    = c("Smoking status", as.character(res$model)),   `N`     = c("", paste0(res$n, " / ", res$n_events)),   p_value = c(NA_real_, res$p_value),   check.names = FALSE )  p <- plot_forest(   data      = df2,   est       = c(NA,   res$HR),   lower     = c(NA,   res$CI_lower),   upper     = c(NA,   res$CI_upper),   ci_column = 3L,   indent    = c(0L,   rep(1L, nrow(res))),   p_cols    = "p_value",   xlim      = c(0.5,  2.5) ) plot(p)

title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: CI appearance Row labels indentation P-value formatting Column headers alignment header renames columns final rendered table. final table always ncol(data) + 2 columns: original columns, plus gap_ci graphic column auto-generated (95% CI) text column. Pass "" gap column position. align controls per-column text alignment across ncol(data) + 2 columns: -1 = left, 0 = centre, 1 = right. NULL (default) left-aligns column 1 centres rest. Background borders Layout saving four formats (PNG, PDF, JPG, TIFF) written 300 dpi white background. function returns plot object invisibly; display plot(p) grid::grid.draw(p).
code: # uses df, est, lower, upper from the minimal example above p <- plot_forest(   data      = df,   est       = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column = 3L,   ci_col    = c("grey50", "steelblue", "steelblue"),  # per-row colours   ci_sizes  = 0.5,       # point size   ci_Theight = 0.15,     # cap height   ref_line  = 1,         # reference line (use 0 for beta coefficients)   xlim      = c(0.2, 5), ticks_at = c(0.5, 1, 2, 3) ) # indent = 0 → bold parent row; indent >= 1 → indented sub-row (plain) p <- plot_forest(   data       = df,   est        = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column  = 3L,   indent     = c(0L, 1L, 1L),        # parent + 2 sub-rows   bold_label = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)  # explicit control (overrides indent default) ) # p_cols: column names in data that contain raw numeric p-values. # Values < 10^(-p_digits) are displayed as e.g. "<0.001". # bold_p = TRUE bolds all p < p_threshold (default 0.05). p <- plot_forest(   data        = df,   est         = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column   = 3L,   p_cols      = "p_value",   p_digits    = 3L,   bold_p      = TRUE,   p_threshold = 0.05 ) # data has 3 columns → final table has 5 columns (original 3 + gap_ci + OR label) # Layout with ci_column = 3L: item | Cases/N | gap_ci | OR (95% CI) | p_value p <- plot_forest(   data      = df,   est       = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column = 3L,   header    = c("Comparison", "Cases / N", "", "HR (95% CI)", "P-value")   #             col 1          col 2        gap  OR label       col 5 ) p <- plot_forest(   data      = df,   est       = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column = 3L,   align     = c(-1L, 0L, 0L, 0L, 1L)   # label left | Cases/N centre | gap | OR centre | p right ) p <- plot_forest(   data       = df,   est        = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column  = 3L,   background = "zebra",       # "zebra" | "bold_label" | "none"   bg_col     = "#F0F0F0",     # shading colour   border     = "three_line",  # "three_line" | "none"   border_width = 3            # scalar or length-3 vector (top / mid / bottom) ) # uses df, est, lower, upper from the minimal example above p <- plot_forest(   data        = df,   est         = est, lower = lower, upper = upper,   ci_column   = 3L,   row_height  = NULL,   # auto (8 / 12 / 10 / 15 mm); or scalar/vector   col_width   = NULL,   # auto (rounds up to nearest 5 mm)   save        = TRUE,   dest        = "forest_main",   # extension ignored; all 4 formats saved   save_width  = 20,              # cm   save_height = NULL             # auto: nrow(data) * 0.9 + 3 cm )

code: plot_tableone(   data    = trial,   vars    = c("age", "marker", "grade", "stage"),   strata  = "trt",   label   = list(age ~ "Age (years)", marker ~ "Marker level (ng/mL)"),   add_p   = TRUE,    # Wilcoxon / chi-squared p-values; formatted as <0.001   add_smd = TRUE,   overall = TRUE,   dest    = "table1",   save    = TRUE )

title: Publication-Ready Visualisation
text: Variable types statistics SMD column SMD column summarises covariate balance groups: - Continuous variables: Cohen’s d (pooled-SD formula) - Categorical variables: RMSD group proportions Excluding rows Use exclude_labels remove specific level rows rendered table (e.g. redundant reference category “Unknown” level): Export formats save = TRUE, four files written simultaneously: PDF PNG rendering requires pagedown webshot2 respectively. Install install.packages(c("pagedown", "webshot2")).
code: dt <- as.data.frame(ops_toy(scenario = "association"))  plot_tableone(   data      = dt,   vars      = c("p21022", "p21001_i0", "p31", "p20116_i0"),   strata    = "dm_status",   type      = list(p21022 = "continuous2"),   # show median + IQR   statistic = list(     all_continuous()  ~ "{mean} ({sd})",     all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"   ),   digits    = list(p21022 ~ 1, p21001_i0 ~ 1),   missing   = "ifany",   # show missing counts when present   save      = FALSE ) plot_tableone(   data    = dt,   vars    = c("p21022", "p21001_i0", "p31"),   strata  = "dm_status",   add_smd = TRUE,   save    = FALSE ) plot_tableone(   data           = dt,   vars           = c("p31", "p20116_i0"),   strata         = "dm_status",   exclude_labels = "Never",   # e.g. remove reference category from display   save           = FALSE )

title: Smoking and Lung Cancer Risk: A Complete Analysis Workflow
text: Cigarette smoking leading preventable cause lung cancer, accounting approximately 85% cases conferring 15–30 times higher risk compared never-smokers. Risk scales cumulative exposure decreases — never fully reverts — cessation. Sex, age, socioeconomic deprivation well-established modifiers association large prospective cohorts UK Biobank. vignette uses simulated UK Biobank-style dataset walk complete ukbflow pipeline canonical exposure– outcome pair.

title: Smoking and Lung Cancer Risk: A Complete Analysis Workflow
text: use package built-function ops_toy() simulate generate synthetic UK Biobank-style dataset analysis.
code: library(ukbflow)  data <- ops_toy(n = 500000, seed = 2026) #> ✔ ops_toy: 500000 participants | 75 columns | scenario = "cohort" | seed = 2026

title: Smoking and Lung Cancer Risk: A Complete Analysis Workflow
text: real RAP session, decode_names() translates UKB field IDs human-readable snake_case column names (e.g. p31 → sex, p21022 → age_at_recruitment).
code: data <- decode_names(data) #> Field dictionary not cached - calling `extract_ls()` to populate it. #> Using dataset: "XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX" #> Fetching approved fields... (cached after first call) #> 29951 fields available. Assign to a variable or use pattern= to search. #> ✔ Renamed 68 columns. #> ! 5 column names longer than 60 characters - consider renaming manually: #> • interpolated_year_when_non_cancer_illness_first_diagnosed_i0_a0 #> • interpolated_year_when_non_cancer_illness_first_diagnosed_i0_a1 #> • interpolated_year_when_non_cancer_illness_first_diagnosed_i0_a2 #> • interpolated_year_when_non_cancer_illness_first_diagnosed_i0_a3 #> • interpolated_year_when_non_cancer_illness_first_diagnosed_i0_a4

title: Smoking and Lung Cancer Risk: A Complete Analysis Workflow
text: first handle non-informative missing codes (e.g. “know”, “Prefer answer”) derive_missing(). Next, convert categorical columns factors derive_covariate(). bin BMI Townsend Deprivation Index (TDI) categories using derive_cut(). Self-reported lung cancer (field 20001) derived derive_selfreport(), searches cancer self-report columns matching label. ICD-10 diagnoses UK Biobank can ascertained four sources: First Occurrence fields, HES inpatient records, death registry, cancer registry. malignant neoplasms lung cancer, cancer registry provides complete accurate ascertainment therefore primary source. derive_case() merges self-report ICD-10 flags single case status earliest date using rule across sources. Finally, derive_timing() classifies case prevalent (disease baseline) incident (disease baseline), determines eligibility survival analysis. derive_followup() computes follow-end date time years participant, taking earliest event date, death date, administrative censoring date.
code: data <- derive_missing(data) #> ✔ derive_missing: replaced 279650 values across 3 columns (action = "na"). data <- derive_covariate(   data,   as_factor = c(     "p31",        # sex     "p20116_i0",  # smoking_status_i0     "p1558_i0",   # alcohol_intake_frequency_i0     "p54_i0"      # uk_biobank_assessment_centre_i0   ) ) #> ── Factor ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> sex [2 levels] #>   Female: n=270191 (54%)  |  Male: n=229809 (46%)  |  <NA>: n=0 (0%) #> smoking_status_i0 [3 levels] #>   Current: n=69867 (14%)  |  Never: n=260458 (52.1%)  |  Previous: n=154871 (31%)  |  <NA>: n=14804 (3%) #> ! alcohol_intake_frequency_i0: 6 levels > max_levels (5), consider collapsing categories. #>   Daily or almost daily: n=40113 (8%)  |  Never: n=45207 (9%)  |  ...  |  <NA>: n=14973 (3%) #> ! uk_biobank_assessment_centre_i0: 10 levels > max_levels (5), consider collapsing categories. #>   Birmingham: n=49709 (9.9%)  |  Bristol: n=49832 (10%)  |  ...  |  Sheffield: n=50293 (10.1%) data <- derive_cut(   data,   col    = "p21001_i0",                              # body_mass_index_bmi_i0   n      = 4,   breaks = c(18.5, 25, 30),   labels = c("Underweight", "Normal", "Overweight", "Obese"),   name   = "bmi_cat" ) #> ── Source: body_mass_index_bmi_i0 ─────────────────────────────────────────── #>   mean=26.21, median=26.19, sd=5.48, Q1=22.48, Q3=29.9, NA=0% (n=0) #> ── New column: bmi_cat ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> bmi_cat [4 levels] #>   Underweight: n=40238 (8%)  |  Normal: n=166710 (33.3%) #>   Overweight:  n=170794 (34.2%)  |  Obese: n=122258 (24.5%)  |  <NA>: n=0 (0%)  data <- derive_cut(   data,   col    = "p22189",                                 # townsend_deprivation_index_at_recruitment   n      = 4,   labels = c("Q1 (least deprived)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (most deprived)"),   name   = "tdi_cat" ) #> ── Source: townsend_deprivation_index_at_recruitment ──────────────────────── #>   mean=-1.25, median=-1.3, sd=3.1, Q1=-3.46, Q3=0.86, NA=0% (n=0) #> ── New column: tdi_cat ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> tdi_cat [4 levels] #>   Q1 (least deprived): n=125290 (25.1%)  |  Q2: n=124831 (25%) #>   Q3: n=125102 (25%)  |  Q4 (most deprived): n=124777 (25%)  |  <NA>: n=0 (0%) data <- derive_selfreport(   data,   name  = "lung_cancer",   regex = "lung cancer",   field = "cancer" ) #> ✔ derive_selfreport (lung_cancer): 6700 cases, 6700 with dates. data <- derive_icd10(   data,   name      = "lung",   icd10     = "^C3[34]",   match     = "regex",   source    = "cancer_registry",   behaviour = 3L ) #> ✔ derive_cancer_registry (lung): 2449 cases, 2449 with date. #> ✔ derive_icd10 (lung): 2449 cases across 1 source, 2449 with date. data <- derive_case(   data,   name                = "lung",   selfreport_col      = "lung_cancer_selfreport",   selfreport_date_col = "lung_cancer_selfreport_date" ) #> ✔ derive_case (lung): 9110 cases, 9110 with date. #> ℹ   Both sources (lung_icd10 & lung_cancer_selfreport): 39 data <- derive_timing(data, name = "lung", baseline_col = "p53_i0")  # date_of_attending_assessment_centre_i0 #> ✔ derive_timing (lung_timing): #> ℹ   0 (no disease): 490890 #> ℹ   1 (prevalent):  5056 #> ℹ   2 (incident):   4054 #> ℹ   NA (no date):   0 data <- derive_followup(   data,   name         = "lung",   event_col    = "lung_date",   baseline_col = "p53_i0",         # date_of_attending_assessment_centre_i0   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-10-31"),   death_col    = "p40000_i0",      # date_of_death_i0   lost_col     = FALSE             # lost-to-follow-up not available in this dataset ) #> ✔ derive_followup (lung): #> ℹ   lung_followup_end: 500000 / 500000 non-missing #> ℹ   lung_followup_years: mean=13.71, median=14.09, range=[0, 16.83]

text: exclude prevalent lung cancer cases participants missing exposure follow-time arrive final analysis cohort. ops_snapshot() handy utility recording cohort size step — snapshot history can directly inform flow diagram methods section. Exclude participants prevalent lung cancer (diagnosed baseline) — eligible incident analysis. Exclude participants missing values exposure covariate. Review full exclusion history. running association analysis, take quick look final cohort — exposure distribution, outcome ascertainment, follow-time.

title: ukbflow
text: ukbflow provides streamlined, RAP-native R workflow UK Biobank analysis — phenotype extraction disease derivation association analysis publication-quality figures. functions designed run within UK Biobank Research Analysis Platform (RAP), compliance 2024+ data policy requiring individual-level data remain cloud.

title: ukbflow
text: Requirements: R ≥ 4.1 · dxpy (dx-toolkit, required RAP interaction)
code: # Recommended pak::pkg_install("evanbio/ukbflow")  # or remotes::install_github("evanbio/ukbflow") pip install dxpy

title: ukbflow
text: Connection — Authenticate RAP via dx-toolkit manage project selection (auth_login, auth_select_project) Data Access — Retrieve phenotype data UKB dataset RAP; monitor asynchronous jobs (fetch_metadata, extract_batch, job_wait) Data Processing — Harmonize multi-source records derive analysis-ready cohort: decode field IDs value codes, build ICD-10 case definitions, compute follow-time (decode_names, decode_values, derive_icd10, derive_followup, derive_case) Association Analysis — Cox, logistic, linear regression automatic three-model adjustment framework; subgroup analysis, dose-response trend, Fine-Gray competing risks (assoc_coxph, assoc_logistic, assoc_subgroup) Genomic Scoring — Distributed plink2 scoring RAP worker nodes: BGEN → PGEN conversion, multi-chromosome GRS computation, standardisation (grs_bgen2pgen, grs_score, grs_standardize) Visualization — Publication-ready forest plots Table 1, saved major formats 300 dpi (plot_forest, plot_tableone) Utilities — Verify environment analysis; generate synthetic UKB-like data development; diagnose missing values; track cohort changes across pipeline steps; exclude withdrawn participants (ops_setup, ops_toy, ops_na, ops_snapshot, ops_withdraw)

title: ukbflow
code: library(ukbflow)  # Simulate UKB-style data locally (on RAP: replace with extract_batch() + job_wait()) data <- ops_toy(n = 5000, seed = 2026) |>   derive_missing()  # Derive lung cancer outcome (ICD-10 C34) and follow-up time data <- data |>   derive_icd10(name = "lung", icd10 = "C34",                source = c("cancer_registry", "hes")) |>   derive_followup(name         = "lung",                   event_col    = "lung_icd10_date",                   baseline_col = "p53_i0",                   censor_date  = as.Date("2022-10-31"),                   death_col    = "p40000_i0")  # Define exposure: ever vs. never smoker data[, smoking_ever := factor(   ifelse(p20116_i0 == "Never", "Never", "Ever"),   levels = c("Never", "Ever") )]  # Cox regression: smoking → lung cancer (3-model adjustment) res <- assoc_coxph(data,   outcome_col  = "lung_icd10",   time_col     = "lung_followup_years",   exposure_col = "smoking_ever",   covariates   = c("p21022", "p31", "p22189"))  # Forest plot res_df <- as.data.frame(res) plot_forest(   data      = res_df,   est       = res_df$HR,   lower     = res_df$CI_lower,   upper     = res_df$CI_upper,   ci_column = 2L )

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
text: Fits Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard model (via survival::finegray() + weighted coxph()) exposure variable returns tidy result table subdistribution hazard ratios (SHR).

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
code: assoc_competing(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   compete_col = NULL,   event_val = 1L,   compete_val = 2L,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95 )  assoc_fg(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   compete_col = NULL,   event_val = 1L,   compete_val = 2L,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95 )

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Primary event column. Mode : multi-value column (integer character codes). Mode B: 0/1 binary column. time_col (character) Follow-time column (numeric, e.g. years). exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. compete_col (character NULL) Mode B : name 0/1 competing event column. NULL (default), Mode used. event_val Scalar value outcome_col indicating primary event (Mode ). Default: 1L. compete_val Scalar value outcome_col indicating competing event (Mode ). Default: 2L. covariates (character NULL) Covariate names Fully adjusted model. NULL, Unadjusted (Age/sex adjusted base = TRUE) run. base (logical) Whether auto-detect age sex columns include Age sex adjusted model. Default: TRUE. conf_level (numeric) Confidence level SHR intervals. Default: 0.95.

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination: exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name returned coxph. model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. n Participants model (NA removal). n_events Primary events analysis set. n_compete Competing events analysis set. SHR Subdistribution hazard ratio. CI_lower Lower CI bound. CI_upper Upper CI bound. p_value Robust z-test p-value weighted Cox. SHR_label Formatted string, e.g. "1.23 (1.05-1.44)".

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
text: Two input modes supported depending outcome coded dataset: Mode - single multi-value column compete_col = NULL (default). outcome_col contains event codes one column (e.g. 0/1/2/3). Use event_val compete_val identify event interest competing event; values treated censored. Example: UKB censoring_type 1 = event, 2 = death (competing), 0/3 = censored. Mode B - dual binary columns compete_col name separate 0/1 column competing event. outcome_col 0/1 column primary event. 1 participant, primary event takes priority. Example: outcome_col = "copd_status", compete_col = "death_status". Internally modes converted three-level factor c("censor", "event", "compete") passed finegray(). Three adjustment models produced (data allow): Unadjusted - always included. Age sex adjusted - base = TRUE age/sex columns detected. Fully adjusted - covariates non-NULL.

title: Fine-Gray competing risks association analysis — assoc_competing
code: # \donttest{ dt <- ops_toy(scenario = "association", n = 500) #> ✔ ops_toy: 500 participants | 33 columns | scenario = "association" | seed = 42 dt <- dt[dm_timing != 1L & htn_timing != 1L]  res <- assoc_competing(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   compete_col  = "htn_status",   covariates   = c("bmi_cat", "tdi_cat") ) #> ℹ Mode B: dm_status (event) + htn_status (compete) #> ℹ Events: 22, Competing: 45, Censored: 317 #> ℹ Exposure: p20116_i0 #> ℹ   Model: Unadjusted #> ℹ   Model: Age and sex adjusted #> ℹ   Model: Fully adjusted #> ✔ Done: 6 result rows across 1 exposure and 3 models. # }

what: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
text: Fits one Cox models exposure variable returns tidy result table suitable downstream forest plots. default, two standard adjustment models always included alongside user-specified model:

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
code: assoc_coxph(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95 )  assoc_cox(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95 )

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. Must contain columns referenced outcome_col, time_col, exposure_col. outcome_col (character) Name event indicator column. Accepts logical (TRUE/FALSE) numeric/integer (0/1). time_col (character) Name follow-time column (numeric, consistent units, e.g. years). exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. variable analysed separately; results stacked row-wise. covariates (character NULL) Additional covariate column names Fully adjusted model (e.g. c("tdi", "smoking", paste0("pc", 1:10))). NULL (default), Fully adjusted model run. base (logical) TRUE (default), always include Unadjusted Age sex adjusted models addition user-specified covariates model. Set FALSE run Fully adjusted model (requires covariates non-NULL). strata (character NULL) Optional stratification variable. Passed survival::strata() Cox formula. conf_level (numeric) Confidence level hazard ratio intervals. Default: 0.95.

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination, following columns: exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name returned coxph (e.g. "bmi_categoryObese" factor, variable name numeric/binary exposures). model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. n Number participants included model (NA removal). n_events Number events model's analysis set (NA removal). person_years Total person-years follow-model's analysis set (rounded, NA removal). HR Hazard ratio (point estimate). CI_lower Lower bound confidence interval. CI_upper Upper bound confidence interval. p_value Wald test p-value. HR_label Formatted string, e.g. "1.23 (1.05-1.44)".

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
text: Unadjusted - covariates (crude). Age sex adjusted - age + sex auto-detected data via UKB field IDs (21022 31). Skipped warning either column found. Fully adjusted - covariates supplied via covariates argument. run covariates non-NULL. Outcome coding: outcome_col may logical (TRUE/FALSE) integer/numeric (0/1). Logical values converted integer internally. Exposure types supported: Binary - 0/1 TRUE/FALSE; produces one term row per model. Factor - produces one term row per non-reference level. Numeric (continuous) - produces one term row per model.

title: Cox proportional hazards association analysis — assoc_coxph
code: dt <- ops_toy(scenario = "association", n = 500) #> ✔ ops_toy: 500 participants | 33 columns | scenario = "association" | seed = 42 dt <- dt[dm_timing != 1L]  res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   covariates   = c("bmi_cat", "tdi_cat"),   base         = FALSE ) #> ℹ outcome_col dm_status: logical detected, converting TRUE/FALSE -> 1/0 #>  #> ── assoc_coxph ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ 1 exposure x 1 model = 1 Cox regression #> ℹ Input cohort: 463 participants (n/n_events/person_years reflect each model's actual analysis set) #>  #> ── p20116_i0 ── #>  #> ✔   Fully adjusted | p20116_i0Previous: HR 0.39 (0.13-1.16), p = 0.0918 #> ✔   Fully adjusted | p20116_i0Current: HR 0.41 (0.09-1.77), p = 0.232 #> ✔ Done: 2 result rows across 1 exposure and 1 model.  # Fully adjusted only (skip Unadjusted + Age-sex) res <- assoc_coxph(   data         = dt,   outcome_col  = "dm_status",   time_col     = "dm_followup_years",   exposure_col = "p20116_i0",   covariates   = c("p21022", "p31", "bmi_cat", "tdi_cat"),   base         = FALSE ) #> ℹ outcome_col dm_status: logical detected, converting TRUE/FALSE -> 1/0 #>  #> ── assoc_coxph ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ 1 exposure x 1 model = 1 Cox regression #> ℹ Input cohort: 463 participants (n/n_events/person_years reflect each model's actual analysis set) #>  #> ── p20116_i0 ── #>  #> ✔   Fully adjusted | p20116_i0Previous: HR 0.41 (0.14-1.21), p = 0.105 #> ✔   Fully adjusted | p20116_i0Current: HR 0.42 (0.10-1.84), p = 0.253 #> ✔ Done: 2 result rows across 1 exposure and 1 model.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
text: Tests proportional hazards (PH) assumption using Schoenfeld residuals via cox.zph. Re-fits models assoc_coxph (interface) returns tidy result table term-level global test statistics.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
code: assoc_coxph_zph(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL )  assoc_zph(   data,   outcome_col,   time_col,   exposure_col,   covariates = NULL,   base = TRUE,   strata = NULL )

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
text: data (data.frame data.table) Analysis dataset. outcome_col (character) Binary event indicator (0/1 TRUE/FALSE). time_col (character) Follow-time column name. exposure_col (character) One exposure variable names. covariates (character NULL) Covariates Fully adjusted model. Default: NULL. base (logical) Include Unadjusted Age sex adjusted models. Default: TRUE. strata (character NULL) Optional stratification variable.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
text: data.table one row per exposure \(\times\) term \(\times\) model combination, columns: exposure Exposure variable name. term Coefficient name. model Ordered factor: Unadjusted < Age sex adjusted < Fully adjusted. chisq Schoenfeld residual chi-squared statistic. df Degrees freedom. p_value P-value PH test (term-level). ph_satisfied Logical; TRUE p_value > 0.05. global_chisq Global chi-squared whole model. global_df Global degrees freedom. global_p Global p-value whole model.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph
text: non-significant p-value (p > 0.05) indicates PH assumption satisfied term. global test (global_p) reflects overall PH assumption whole model.

title: Proportional hazards assumption test for Cox regression — assoc_coxph_zph

text: Stratifies dataset single grouping variable () runs specified association model (coxph, logistic, linear) within subgroup. Unlike assoc_coxph siblings, automatic age--sex-adjusted model: subgroup level variable defines stratum zero variance, making auto-detected adjustment meaningless. Accordingly, function accept base argument. Instead, two models available:
